text: This meeting is being recorded.
How's it going, Tyler?
Hey, Katherine.
Going well.
Nice to meet you.
Nice to meet you, too.
Having a good day so far?

It kind of feels like a Monday, but it's just been very busy at GrowthX lately, and, you know, maybe the day after the Super Bowl is the worst Monday, and then the day after that is like a normal Monday, so it's like a normal Monday, in short.
No, I feel you.
I feel like I'm running around like crazy this week all of a sudden, but yeah, excited to dig in with you.
And
And hear more about the role and answer any questions you might have about me and my experience.
Yeah, definitely.
And thanks so much for sending that email over.
That was just like great context to get to know you as a person.
And I'm not sure if you saw my email back, but I love the part of the country that you're based in.
And it's like the Pacific Northwest is amazing as it is.
And that Mount Hood and Columbia River Gorge area is the best.
I know.
I feel so lucky to live here and feel like I'm living the dream every day.
But I'm curious what got you interested in it because I don't normally hear of that as a bachelor party location.
So you're right there.
It's not a casino, you know, it's not some crazy place where there's lots of clubs.
I lived in San Francisco for three years and it was like my favorite place.
I lived, I'd been to Seattle a bunch.
I'd never been to Oregon.
So I just wanted to like go to that state and then combining like nature with just hanging out with my friends for the opening weekend of college football.
was like the perfect place to be, to get outside, but also to just hang out at a cool Airbnb.
Nice.
Very cool.
So did you already get married?
Did it already happen or is it still ending?
It's in April.
So final stretch.
Maybe that's why I'm tired.
I went to a vendor yesterday, like at 3 p.m., which is like not the timing that you want to go leave your sales job and visit a wedding vendor.
But I got to do like two full-time jobs right now to get us finalized and let our wedding planner take over after that.
Nice.
That's cool.
Where are you doing it?
We're doing it local.
Yeah, we're both from Houston.
So just making it easy on our friends and family, but I'm doing this.
Ceremony at the Botanic Gardens and then the reception at like the school kind of like venue in the middle of the city.
Yeah.
Oh, cool.
Awesome.
Great.
Well, yeah, definitely have some questions for you.
But the general idea is just to like get to know you more, get to know your background in sales and talk about some deals that you've worked on in particular.
Would love to just like start off and kind of elaborate on your email.
Right.
Like tell me a little bit more just about your background in sales and what has you interested in growthx in particular in terms of making a switch potentially.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
When Tucker reached out, I got kind of that like tingly feeling, you know, when you realize there's a pretty special opportunity.
So that was kind of what what sparked it.
I mean, but yeah, a little history about me.
I started off my career as an SDR.
At a small software company in Portland.
Then I went into more like channel sales from there, working with like resellers and partners.
And then I moved over to Ireland briefly with my now husband.
But when I was there, it was during COVID, things were weird, but I ended up joining Hotjar, which I'm not sure if you're familiar with Hotjar.
I've definitely heard of them.
Yeah.
Heat mapping is what Hotjar was originally known for.
Exactly.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Heat mapping.
So, but yeah, they had just kind of a cool vibe of being sort of the startup, you know, with a really good remote work culture, you know, people from all around the world.
And what was especially cool was their sales motion, which instead of, you know, having to do all this cold outreach, they really focused on having like product-led growth.
Yeah.
And the founder, you know, was building in public.

He had worked at a conversion rate optimization agency and then went out on his own when he realized that there was this problem in the market of not having visibility into how users were really flowing through websites.
So I got to join when they were trying to scale up the sales team from just mostly online self-serve to, hey, how can we layer in sales reps to help customers pick the right plan?
Because people were sort of self-serving into the lowest plan.
So we would reach out.
We'd do some of that like warm, I guess, like warm outbound.

I don't know if that's what you'd really call it, but the people who had just created accounts, inviting them to a call and then sort of taking them through and showing them how to get value from the platform and recommending a plan and all that.
So it was a really fun time when we got to kind of build out the sales process, hire a bunch of reps.
And just really flesh things out.
And then within that, I had a couple different roles.
So I was focused on new business at the start.
Then I moved into sort of like an expansion-focused role.
And then for a while, I was working exclusively with agency partners and building out our sales through our agency motion where people would recommend us into their clients.
And we'd give them a commission for referring them.
And then in the backdrop of all of that, we were acquired by a larger company called Content Square.
So I've also been navigating, you know, being acquired and all the joys that come with integrating companies.
And they also acquired Heap Analytics.
So we've been having a fun merging journey.
So that's the long story.
Yeah.
Yeah, super, super cool.
I can connect, I can relate to a lot of that.
Stensys, it's like a digital asset management platform, right?
Yes, yes, because I heard you were kind of in the creative space in the start, maybe?
Yeah, AIR, they didn't know if they wanted to be digital asset management or creative operations, and we're trying to like build for both.
And so that can be hard to, you know, fit in for like enterprise use cases where integrations are important with the data management, you know, being such a critical piece.
But then also like trying to build a platform where people can upload versions using a desktop app.
more like creatives, right?
And then approve things and comment and leave granular feedback.
And so it was a great experience and AIR is still doing well.
But yeah, they weren't sure what they wanted to be for a while.
And dams require a lot of functionality for the enterprise, as I'm sure you know.
Yes, yes.
No, and Extensys.
Like, was a pre-dot-com bubble company.
So they, and then they were acquired by this Japanese company.

And then they were trying to figure out, like, that move from on-prem to cloud services, which was really interesting to be a part of and see that shift in an older tech company and how they were navigating that.
So, yeah, definitely seen.
I feel like change is, like, the headline of my career so far in, like, navigating change.
So, yeah, I guess, like, from, like, legacy to modern and then also from startup to being a part of an enterprise.
Like, you kind of seen both directions happen.
And I was at Segment when they got acquired by Twilio, and that's when I decided to leave.
So it was sort of a similar situation with your story as well.
Though I know it was more like the opportunity seemed really enticing versus, like, you were actively looking.
But that's great.
that's great.
I'd love to just kind of like dig, just dive right in and like understand like how do you position the value like of what you're selling to a CMO or a go-to-market leader?

Not like in a particular deal, just like generally like help me sort of, because I don't know, you know, Content Square and Hotjar perfectly, but like how do you position the value of those products to a marketing leader?
Yeah, so I think where we really come in is typically there's some kind of trigger.
So maybe they're rebranding, they're redoing their website, or they're driving a lot of traffic to their website, and they're realizing that metrics just aren't where you want them to be.
The conversion rate isn't where you want it to be.
And traditional analytics tools like a Google Analytics, they tell you like what happened, but not really like why, and what people were doing.
Once they got to the website.
So how do you go about improving?
Well, you need visibility into what people are doing on the site.
Where are they engaging?
Where do they drop off?
What do they completely ignore?
So that you can improve and then optimize.
So we really fit into that like continuous optimization cycle that you might need.

So I think the value really comes in when we're talking about getting the most out of all that hard work that your team is doing to drive traffic to the website and build content and making sure that it's actually landing and generating the results that you're looking for.
Okay, nice.
And I guess what alternatives do they have if they were looking to go beyond Google Analytics, but, you know, weren't like, I want to buy, I want to buy content square hot jar right away.
I guess they could like spend more time.

I think there was a big move when Google Analytics changed over to Google Analytics 4, and a lot of companies didn't really set up their Google Analytics properly, so they don't really trust their data that they have.

So I guess they could invest more time in building that out, but it requires a lot of manual resources, and then anytime you update something new, you have to add more tags, get developers involved, and kind of keep that cycle going.
So that's one option.
The other option would just be trying different things in the dark and seeing what sticks, I suppose.
Or, of course, you could check out one of our competitors, too, and get some other analytics tools in place, too.
Who are the top two or three competitors that you'll run into most frequently?
Let's see.
So a big one is Amplitude.
More recently.
recently.
We're seeing PostHog, which I think you guys have, I noticed, on your site and your platforms.
Let's see.
Adobe Analytics is another one that comes up.
Full Story.
So there's a lot of different tools in the marketplace that do bits and pieces of different things.

And I would say we position ourselves as being a, I hate to say it, but all in one, you know, where you can have, you know, your marketing team, your product team, your engineering team, and kind of appealing to lots of different teams with our different capabilities that we have.
Makes sense.
And I can see how with the acquisitions, it really is end to end, right?
Like, I don't think there's heat mapping in those other solutions.
Maybe there is.
But is that true?
Some of them have, you know, some light heat mapping.
Um, Um,
But yeah, coming at it from different angles, I would say.
Yeah, outside of like the all-in-one and reason I'm kind of going deep on this is we have competitors come up quite a bit.
You know, we have to like justify how we're differentiated, why we have a premium in addition to all-in-one.
Like take me a little deeper into how you'd win a deal over like one of those competitors.
Oh, yeah.
I mean, I feel like proof, if I'm thinking in terms of growthx, but also in our case, I think a lot of folks come to us having used us in the past and had a really good experience.
And I think our like ease of use comes up a lot.
So, you know, you can have a great analytics tool, but if it's really hard to set up and really hard to use, then, you know, nobody uses it and you don't get results from it.
So, especially on the hot jar side, we have more solutions.

It's part of ContentSquare, which things have gotten a little bit muddled, but I think like the original value prop of Hotjar was just you install one tag and then you're off to the races and you can immediately be getting insights with little to no lift.
There's also probably like a security component since we're dealing with user behavior data, legal and privacy come up quite a bit.
We're a European company, so I talk about some of our protections there quite a bit.
Let's see.
Yeah, those are the big things that come to mind, but case studies, examples of how others have done it, and then our trial process.
I think it just making, really it all comes down to just making it super easy because I think a lot of our competitors will gatekeep their pricing and they're not very transparent.
And so we just say, hey, get in there, get using it, get started.
And then once it's on their site and they're.
Getting value from it, it's hard to kind of displace us, I would say.
Yeah, it makes sense.
Case studies are so important, aren't they?
Even if like, you know, you got the best product in the world, everyone always is going to ask you for, okay, well, what could I expect to drive in terms of traffic, right?
What have you done for another client?
It's kind of crazy.
I feel like unless you're like a super big product expert without a case study or without a proof point, you might struggle to win the deal.
What like metrics or outcomes would you connect the solution or would you connect ContentSquare, Hotjar?
Like what metrics or outcomes would you connect to like their growth strategy with your solution, basically?
Yeah, it depends on the use case because we can be on a marketing site, but also on like a product, a digital product as well, but for the marketing.
Use case, it really comes down to conversion rate, I would say.
We have a few jobs to be done that we did some research around, so they fall into a couple different categories, but finding friction on the website and figuring out where you can improve.

And then a lot of people, I think, come to us because they feel like there's lots of opinions flying around, and maybe the CEO wants them to do one thing, and other members of the team want them to do another thing, and they want to have data to back up their decisions and say, hey, this content that you wanted us to create, no one's landing on it, no one's engaging with it, no one's scrolling past the fold.
Like, we need to do something different.
So it's not really, I guess it's not like a tangible metric necessarily, but it's having that data to help them back up their decisions and feel more confident, but ultimate result.
What want for clients is an improved conversion rate, and that's really what it all comes down to.
What about on the product use case?
That makes sense, yeah.
Yeah, product use case, I think we see more folks who are like UX folks who maybe they are noticing that adoption isn't where they want it to be.

They want to understand maybe a new feature launch and how that's going, so they'll watch sessions of users as they interact with that new feature to see how it's going, because your metrics might tell you it's going okay, but then you actually notice there's some huge friction that's slowing people down as they try to go through a certain flow, so things like that.
So I think that ties back to brand and just digital experience, like ease of use, I'm trying to think of the right word for this.

But like adoption, kind of like, yeah, just like perception of the users as they go through, like, how was their experience, you know, do did they, you know, have a good time, I guess, using your your platform, or did they experience frustration, which I think ties back to like, your overall brand perception, and then, you know, your ability to keep customers and prevent churn.
Makes sense, makes sense.
Who would be like the buyer, typically?
Like, would you be selling to like a VP of marketing or a head of demand gen?
Or who would be the person that could pull the trigger?
Yeah, VP of marketing, typically, we see demand gen, we see product marketing leaders as well.
Okay, nice.
Really across like the marketing, um, org.
And like you mentioned that when you, um, when you were working with a company, you like helped build out their product led motion.
And some of the problem was that they would just go to like.
Like the cheapest plan that was out there.
I guess help me understand the work that you did to solve that problem and what some of the impact was to the changes that you all rolled out for the product-led sales-assisted motion.
Yeah, I think it all came back to our pricing model.
So it's based on the number of sessions that we're going to collect from their website on a daily basis.
And we had basically the model was you can choose how many sessions you collect.
So most people would start at our base plan of like 500 sessions per day.
And we would sample their traffic if they went over that, right?
So it wouldn't just cut them off, but it would sort of sprinkle them throughout the day.
And people didn't necessarily realize that they weren't capturing the full breadth of like the data that they had available.
So we had to find ways.
Is to find out which customers had like the biggest gap, I guess.
So like they're on the 500 plan, but maybe they're getting 10,000 sessions a day or, you know, 30,000 sessions a day.
Those were the biggest opportunities for us to upsell.
And then I kind of we called it like stealth sales, but kind of going in to meet with them and show them how to get more value out of the platform, show them how to use it.

And then through there sort of like peppering in messaging around the risks of not collecting all of their data and making decisions based off of only a small sample size and then positioning a plan from there.
So I think the overall kind of building out of that process was like identifying the opportunities.
And we use I like to use like our product analytics data to find high potential accounts within our install base and then building out the messaging for that.
And yeah, we had kind of like a training, I guess, and ongoing.
Reinforcement of how to pitch this like 100% capture story was in our sales org.
Super cool.
So it wasn't like y'all were changing the pricing model or anything like that.
It was more so there was a problem.
Everybody was choosing the free plan or the cheapest plan.
And some work was done to stand up the sales process that changed that outcome.
Is that basically what happened?
Yeah, to make them realize like, hey, you know, you can start small on the lowest tier, but you're not going to achieve the results that you're looking for on that plan necessarily.
So helping them build out that business case for full capture and articulate the value of that.
Yeah, it's interesting.
We do a lot here where you're creating from zero, right?
Like we just hired our second salesperson, and they're selling the product that we just launched called Check That, which is like our visibility.
Yeah.
monitoring platform.
And everything is being created from, you know, the first time with Nigel taking the lead on that.
When I joined, you know, we were just selling our services.
And I basically had like the call recordings that Marcel ran as the only enablement for me to run with, which don't get me wrong, it's great enablement, watching the CEO sell what you have to sell.
There's no better blueprint than that.
Um, so, so that's why I'm curious about it, but how did you get like tapped in for this project and like what other salespeople were involved?
Like, was this something specific to you?
Was this a group of people who were working on this?
Um, I guess just help me understand how the project and your involvement came to, came to fruition.
Yeah, I think.
Yeah, would say it's, it was definitely collaborative, for sure.
And, um, when I first joined Hotjar, I, definitely,
Similar to you, I spent most of my time watching call recordings and seeing what the common questions were, where the common pitfalls were, and sort of going, okay, how can I improve upon this?
And how can I make this better?
And what would I do differently?
But I would say this particular project was driven, you know, we had our director of sales that kind of recognized the gap and then kind of like positioned the problem.
And then we as a team sort of worked on like just in the field on calls, figuring out the right way to position and message this.
So I would say it was kind of like trial and error.
We did a lot of like call coaching.
So we do like team call reviews where we'd watch someone's call and sort of critique and see what opportunities there were.
And then we also built out content and slides like showing like.
And here's the difference between full capture, like your heat map with like 5% versus full capture, like, you know, the results are completely different.
So you could make decisions based on like data that is incorrect and examples like that that we could use during sales calls.
Okay, super cool.
Like, sort of like a shared initiative across the team?
Yeah, absolutely.
Yeah.
Let's switch gears a little bit.
Let's move into the outbound side.
Since you started your career as an SDR, I did too.
Good way to start your sales life.
Nothing harder than that.
Yeah.
What's like an experiment or a campaign on the outreach side that you ran that consistently booked meetings?
Tell me more about one of those.
Ooh, um, that's a good question.
So a couple things come to mind, like...
I mean, I do Outbound now in my current role as well.
It's been more of a focus with the shift to ContentSquare.
But at the SDR days, you know, I would do campaigns around, we had like a single user software that we could upgrade to like an enterprise level account.
So I'm focusing on like identifying single users within larger organizations and seeing what potential was there.
So I tend to lean towards like the lowest hanging fruit, I would say, and like the warmer options and avenues.
And then in Hotjar, in the agency world, I was recruiting agencies to join our partner program.
And I had a lot of success reaching out to a lot of companies will post their partner networks online.
Reach out to our, like, competitors, partners, and sort of position us and try to, like, get them on calls, which worked pretty well if they were using, like, a similar solution within their agency.

Or I could look within our database, our install base, and I found agencies that were sitting in multiple client accounts, and I would reach out to them and say, hey, I see that you're, like, involved with a few different clients.
Like, let's talk, and then pitch them on the opportunity to, you know, expand to their wider, wider book of business.
So, things like that.
Yeah, I love that.

mean, at the end of the day, like, we've only got so much time, and if there isn't, like, intention or strategy behind why you're spending the time, especially with Outbound, on a certain group or a certain audience, then you're probably wasting time, right?
So, that resonates around, like, the low-hanging fruit approach.
Because there's got to be some sort of rationale behind why they'd want to meet with you, right?
And all these people have that rationale.
They're working with your existing clients.
They're partnered with a competitor and maybe experiencing some pain.
So I love that.
That's a good example.
Going from sourcing the deals to actually running them, I would love to hear a story about how you closed one of your biggest deals in your career.
Oh, yes.
I anticipated this question.
Similar story.
So I've had a couple with some large pharmaceutical companies because pharma companies tend to have lots of different brands and then lots of different websites.
But they also have lots of different teams, you know, working on those different brands.
And I identified, again, within our install base that we had.
We you know, disparate teams that were paying like for monthly with a credit card subscriptions and started basically outreaching to them to learn how they were using it.
And then these companies tend to have like a center of excellence for like user experience or like digital marketing.
So who's like that central hub that kind of makes decisions about tooling across the organization?
And then the organizations as well, like teams will like pay with the credit card and try to kind of fly under the radar.
So once they were on the radar of like the wider organization, they want to get them, you know, more into a contract and, you know, more like just from like a risk perspective, I suppose.
So, yeah, I had some success basically taking a few disparate teams and then spreading our solution to all their different websites.
And brands and And.
Kind of doing a wider contract for the enterprise.
So that was one that I was particularly proud with, proud of.
Nice.
Was that like one pharmaceutical company in particular?
Or did you take that approach in like multiple scenarios?
You mentioned like you had a couple of large pharmaceutical companies.
So I was just trying to understand, is this example like relevant to all of them or just one of them?
Yeah, there were two.
So there was a, I guess I could, I don't know if I should name names, but, but yeah, there were two.
Whatever you're, whatever you're comfortable with.
Yeah.
worries.
Yeah.
One was Johnson & Johnson.
Yeah.
Before, I guess they were rolling in to become Kenview.
I think they split out some of theirs.
And then there was Gilead Pharmaceuticals, which is based in California, I believe.
So, and then I did a similar thing as well with like a, a gift carding company called Black Hawk Network that they do online gift cards and they work with a ton of different brands.
And, and
had a similar thing where they had accounts spread all over the place and we did like a consolidation multi-year contract to empower more teams.
It makes sense.
If you could take me into the details a bit more with one of the examples in terms of like, how did you build a champion?
How did you like sell, quantify the value or build a business case, if not quantifying the value, right?
How did you, you know, what hurdles came up?
I mean, these companies, like they have a lot of complex procurement processes, no doubt.
Like just help me understand some of the granular details a little bit better on one example.
Yeah, so I think the freshest one is probably this Blackhawk Network deal.
So I would say their teams were using us, but they were running into that issue where they had...
Their plans weren't, like, high enough to capture all of their traffic.
And part of our platform is the ability to launch, like, surveys and gather feedback from your users.
And then you can also tie that back to the actual individual user session.
And so if someone leaves you some negative feedback, you can then see what their journey was and where they got stuck and where they got frustrated.
So their teams were frustrated because they were getting all this negative feedback, but they didn't have enough data to tie it back to those sessions.
So I really drew on that and how they were sort of underpowered to be able to make decisions.
So it was like connecting with those different teams.
And then there was their VP of product who I worked with pretty closely in aligning on, you know, what was the scope, the different websites within the scope and building out a business case.
I am a...
I'm big fan of this guy called Nate Narsala.
don't know if you're familiar with him, but he's one of those LinkedIn influencer type people.
But his big thing is essentially taking what you learn from discovery and the actual words that your prospects use and plugging those into a business case.
Because you can use your own branding and your own company language, but unless you're speaking the language of the organization, then it just doesn't hit as hard, I guess.
And the business case has to be like unbranded.
So it looks like some document that they created internally.
So I like to put these together for my prospects so that they can put their name on it and sort of talk about like, what's the current problem?
What have we tried?
Where do we want to go from here?
What are the potential outcomes?
What are the metrics that we think we're going to be able to influence?
And then what's like the required investment from our part?
So kind of putting that together and then sharing that internally.
What's was part of this?
So.
Yeah.
Super, super cool.
Yeah.
I haven't heard of like creating it non-branded so that it can look like something they created internally, but I like that.
That's logical.
And then using their own words.
I mean, that's key.
And sometimes they have short-term memory loss, right?
Around what they told you on the first or second call.
And they're like, I didn't say that.
Or maybe they had a pain point that was like significant, but then they dial it back, you know, when you're getting to a place where it's time to actually make a deal.
So you got to remind them what they said and remind them of that pain, right?
So that they can connect the dots and realize like, I got to solve this problem today, not three months from now, not next quarter.
With the Blackhawk Network deal, what was like your biggest personal accomplishment for that deal in particular?
Like, what are you most proud of?

I I'm most proud of like I feel like the connection that I made with the basically the economic buyer and like she was this sort of like Eastern European vibe and very cold at first, you know, and sort of like hard to break through.

And I feel like through our our work together and persistence and, you know, like we call them like hallway chats, but like talking with her one on one and like getting to know her, she opened up a lot more to me.
And, you know, I just feel like we we ended up building like a strong connection together and like felt like accomplished when we got the deal done together.
Um, so I feel like breaking down her walls and like getting on her good side was something I I was proud of in that.
How'd you get on her good side?
I think it was just like I try to be.
I try to make the prospect feel like they're the only person I'm talking to, you know, and like be extremely responsive, extremely like available to them.
And just, you know, when things would come up, just squashing them really quickly and just being that reliable person that they could work with.
Makes sense.
Like you're on the same side of the table, like you're working on the project with them and not trying to sell them something.
Exactly.
Yes.
Yes, absolutely.
And then what would you say is like your biggest failure in that example?
I don't like the wording of the question.
So like instead of biggest failure, take it as like in hindsight, what would you have done differently?
Ooh, that's a good question on that on that specific deal.
Yeah, I think, I think so, just because we've been talking about it, but you could also like, tell me something you could have, you would
It done differently on one of the Pharma deals as well.
Yeah, I think on this deal, it's a good question.
There's a lot of disparate teams that were involved.
This is the one thing that comes to mind.
So maybe I could have done more work in shoring up the next layer of power, I guess, the layer below.
Because I feel like once procurement got involved, they started going, hey, maybe we don't need it on this one, or maybe we can reduce over here.
And I feel like having built up more support across the different heads of the different...
It was like a regional thing.
They had Europe, they had Americas, and Asia Pacific.
So, you know, shoring up support with some of those folks maybe would have helped with increasing the deal size, potentially.
And the scope of the deal.
So that's something that comes to mind.
mean, right now I'm trying, we have like other solutions.
So I'm trying to like cross sell our other solutions to them.
And, you know, trying to do basically like a roadshow is what I was calling it, where we go and kind of meet with the different departments.
And my contact Yulia was saying like, she can introduce me to different people.
Um, so I'm, I'm trying to build out that support and, uh, certain, certain contexts are need to connect with more.
So, so yeah, that's what comes to mind.
That makes sense.
You know, there's always the healthy tension between you want to get the deal done.
You don't want to lose it.
And then also like, well, if I would have done X, Y, Z, could have closed it for 25 to a hundred percent more.
So why didn't I do that?
Right.
So, um, the good, the good thing is you won the deal.
Exactly.
Yeah.
The last piece of my questions, really, and then I can open it up to any questions you have, is how would your manager have rated you 1 to 10 on that Blackhawk Network deal, do you think?
